title: Space Technology is Redefining Maritime Security in South Asia
gist: Space-based assets are redefining maritime security in the contested Indian Ocean, shifting power away from fleet size and choke points toward domain awareness and real-time detection, and widening South Asian asymmetries as smaller littoral states depend on India, China, or other external powers for surveillance.

insights.claim: Maritime power is no longer confined to fleet size, overseas bases, and control over choke points; modern naval effectiveness increasingly depends on maritime domain awareness, real-time threat detection, and space-based assets.
insights.speaker: Gayathry Gopal
insights.so_what: Reframes naval competitiveness around space and data infrastructure, favoring investment in satellites and surveillance tech over hulls alone.
insights.locator: Maritime power is no longer confined

insights.claim: India has begun integrating space capabilities into its maritime framework while China is expanding its orbital presence over the Indian Ocean, accentuating asymmetries in South Asia.
insights.speaker: Gayathry Gopal
insights.so_what: An orbital arms race over the Indian Ocean creates demand for space-based maritime surveillance capacity.
insights.locator: Recognizing this shift, India has begun

insights.claim: Most South Asian littoral states have limited capacity to independently monitor and respond to maritime threats and rely largely on external powers for surveillance.
insights.speaker: Gayathry Gopal
insights.so_what: Surveillance dependency gives India and China leverage over smaller states, a lever in regional influence competition.
insights.locator: As most South Asian littoral states have limited capacity
